coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient carbon dots for quantitatively visualizing pH fluctuations in cells, zebrafish, mice and tumors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Libo
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: In vivo pH is closely related to complicated physiological and pathological processes. Quantitatively probing pH and visualizing pH variation via fluorescence (FL) imaging technique in living cells and organisms is crucial but difficult to accomplish. Herein, green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized, and their distinct advantages of extraordinarily high fluorescence quantum yield (FQY, 71.4%), unique photostability, high selectivity, sterling biocompatibility, appropriate pKa for biosensing, enable CDs to serve as pH-activatable probes for real time quantitative detection of continuous pH fluctuation in living cells, zebrafish, mice and tumors. We believe CDs are presently among the best-of-breed pH probes for comprehensive biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
pH sensor
Fluorescence imaging
Quantitative detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence Response of CDs to pH Changes
In Vivo pH Measurements in Mice
pH Sensitivity, Stability and Fluorescence Imaging of CDs
In Vitro Fluorescence Imaging of CDs

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023 ), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) High-resolution TEM image of CDs. The inset is the diameter distribution of CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra and (c) FL spectra of CDs. The spectra were measured in neutral ultrapure water. (d) Fluorescence decay curve. (e) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS of (f) C1s and (g) O1s. (h) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

(a) FL spectra of CDs (20 μg/mL) in DHP-CA buffer solutions with different pH (λex = 440 nm). (b) Relationship of FL intensity at 482 nm versus pH, inset: photographs of CDs in different pH environments. (c) pH reversibility of CDs between pH = 4.0 and pH = 7.4. (d) Anti-interference experiment of CDs. (e) FL images of CDs in buffer solutions (pH = 4.0, 4.4, 4.8, 5.4, 5.8, 6.4, 6.8, and 7.4) (λex = 440 nm, λem = 520 nm). (f) Corresponding quantification of FL intensity in (c).

(a) CLSM images of fluorescence response of CDs to pH change in Hela cells. (b) Corresponding average FL intensity vs pH in (a). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. (c) Linear fitting curve between average FL intensity and pH.

(a) CLSM images of zebrafish juvenile treated with CDs (1 mg/mL) solution (pH = 4.0–8.0). (b) Corresponding mean FL intensity of the zebrafish juvenile. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. (c) Calibration curve of FL intensity vs pH in (b). (d) FL images of zebrafish adult treated with CDs (1 mg/mL) solution (pH = 4.0–8.0), λex = 440 nm and λem = 520 nm. (e) Corresponding average FL intensity vs pH in (d).
(a) FL images of mice injected subcutaneously with CDs solutions (1 mg/mL, 100 μL), λex = 440 nm and λem = 520 nm. (b) The corresponding quantification of FL intensity vs pH in (a).
(a) FL images of CDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) injected into normal tissue and tumor tissue. (b) FL intensity quantification in (a). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05.
Sketch maps of the synthesis of CDs and the fluorescence sensing of pH in cells, zebrafish and mice.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient carbon dots for quantitatively visualizing pH fluctuations in cells, zebrafish, mice and tumors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In vivo pH is closely related to complicated physiological and pathological processes. Quantitatively probing pH and visualizing pH variation via fluorescence (FL) imaging technique in living cells and organisms is crucial but difficult to accomplish. Herein, green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized, and their distinct advantages of extraordinarily high fluorescence quantum yield (FQY, 71.4%), unique photostability, high selectivity, sterling biocompatibility, appropriate pKa for biosensing, enable CDs to serve as pH-activatable probes for real time quantitative detection of continuous pH fluctuation in living cells, zebrafish, mice and tumors. We believe CDs are presently among the best-of-breed pH probes for comprehensive biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Maintaining an acid-base balance is vital for regulating various biological processes [1]. The anomalous cellular pH may cause many diseases such as cancers, nervous system problems and fatal diseases [2–4]. Therefore, monitoring pH fluctuation in cells and organisms would supply essential information for lucubrating pH-related physiological and pathological processes. In recent years, fluorescence (FL) imaging is becoming an indispensable pH detection technique for biological research and clinical diagnosis owe to its distinguished merits of high sensitivity, eminent spatial and temporal resolution, convenient noninvasive and real-time monitoring [5,6]. On that account, masses of molecular probes have popped up [7–9]. Nevertheless, most of them are subjected to strong hydrophobicity, insufficient photostability, poor biocompatibility and tedious synthesis.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been diffusely applied in anti-counterfeiting [10–12], sensing [13,14,34,35], bioimaging [15,16,36], nanomedicine [17–23,37], fluorescent ink [24] etc., benefiting from the excellent advantages of favorable optical performance, easy synthesis and functionalization and good cell permeability. Compared to conventional organic dyes and metal-based nanomaterials, CDs have the superiorities of outstanding water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and high photostability [25–27], which are intriguing nanoprobes for bioanalysis and bioimaging. Up to now, some CDs-based pH sensors have been exploited [28–30]. For an instance, Sun et al. prepared silicon-doped CDs as pH-sensitive fluorescent ink for information encryption [24]; Zhang et al. synthesized CDs from ionic liquids for extreme acidity detection of Escherichia coli. [26]; Yang et al. fabricated CDs for wound pH monitoring [31]; Ye et al. constructed CDs for visualizing pH fluctuations in zebrafish [32]. However, there is hardly any work achieved in quantitatively supervising pH changes in living animals and tumors.

Herein, CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method from perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride and citric acid (CA), which emitted green light with the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) as high as 71.4% (Scheme 1 ). CDs was FL-activatable in the pH range of 4.0–8.0. In addition, by virtue of in vivo optical imager and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM), CDs could act as pH indicators to quantitatively monitor pH in living cells, zebrafish and mice. What's more, CDs were further utilized for quantitative pH visualization of tumor. The fascinating features of CDs including high FQY, outstanding reversibility, desirable anti-interference and low toxicity, are conductive to build a handy and efficient platform for pH sensing and imaging.

Citric acid, and disodium hydrogen phosphate were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride was obtaind from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-Diphenyltetr-azolium bromide (MTT) was acquired from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents are of analytical grade and used directly without further purification.

Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (3 mg) was dissolved in 8 mL of NaOH (1 M), then 0.5 g of citric acid (CA) was added to the solution. The mixture was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the crude product was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 8 h. Finally, CDs were freeze-dried to obtain a dark brown solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Response of CDs to pH Changes
ce:section.ce:para: Disodium hydrogen phosphate (DHP) and CA buffer solutions (pH = 4.0–8.0, Table S1) were used to study the impact of pH changes on the luminescence of CDs. First, CDs stock solution (40 μg/mL) was prepared with ultrapure water. Second, DHP-CA buffer solutions were used to dilute the stock solution to 20 μg/mL. Finally, fluorescence spectrophotometer recorded the FL spectra of CDs under 440 nm of excitation.

The cells in the logarithmic growth period were inoculated in a six pore plate at a quantity of 5 × 104 cells/pore and cultured in DMEM for 24 h. Then the DMEM was changed with 1 mL of DMEM including CDs (10 μg/mL), and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. The medium was changed with 1 mL of DMEM with different pH, and continue to incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, and then washed with PBS. 4% of paraformaldehyde solution was added to each well to fix the cells for 10 min. The cells were washed with PBS and observed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 700).

The inseminated AB genotype zebrafish eggs were purchased from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd. Before imaging, the embryos were incubated at 25 °C for 3 d. The 3-day-old zebrafish were treated with CDs (1 mg/mL) at buffer solutions with different pH for 2 h. Then, zebrafish were washed with PBS and imaged with CLSM.

The 4-month-old zebrafish were incubated with CDs (1 mg/mL) at different pH for 2 h and washed with PBS. The PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina Series III was used to record the FL images under the excitation of 440 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vivo pH Measurements in Mice
ce:section.ce:para: Different pH (4.0, 4.4, 4.8, 5.4, 5.8, 6.4, 6.8 and 7.4) of CDs solution (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) was injected subcutaneously into the left lower limb of Kunming mice, and subjected to in vivo optical imager to take photos under 440 nm of excitation.

CDs were acquired from perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride and citric acid (CA) via hydrothermal method. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1a) indicates that CDs have well-distinguished lattice structures and the spacing of the lattice is 0.34 nm, which is accordant with the basic spacing of 002 plane of graphite. The diameter of CDs were revealed by HR-TEM with a size of 4.23 ± 0.86 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs have three characteristic peaks at 210, 440 and 460 nm (Fig. 1b). The former one origins from the π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 hybrid, and the other may be related to the n-π* transitions of C-O/C=O. The FL spectra of CDs (Fig. 1c) display that the two emission bands centred at 487 and 510 nm remained almost unchanged with the excitation wavelength and the maximum emission was obtained under 460 nm of irradiation. The fluorescence decay curve of CDs (Fig. 1d) is mono-exponential and the lifetime is 4.96 ns. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of CDs in water is up to 71.4%, which is presently among the best-in-class in CDs. Three main peaks of C 1 s (285.2 eV), NaKLL (497.4 eV) and O 1 s (531.6 eV) were observed in the full survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs (Fig. 1e). The XPS spectrum of C 1 s was decomposed into four characteristic peaks at 288.2, 286.5, 284.8 and 284.4 eV, which belong to CO, CO, CC and CC, respectively (Fig. 1f). The XPS spectrum of the O 1 s reveals the three peaks at 530.9, 532.1 and 535.2 eV, corresponding to CO, C-O-C and C-OH, respectively (Fig. 1g). FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1h) depicts that there are OH (3300 cm−1), CO (1550 cm−1), CC (1380 cm−1), CO (1280 cm−1) groups on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH Sensitivity, Stability and Fluorescence Imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The study on the fluorescence response of CDs to pH were conducted in disodium hydrogen phosphate (DHP) and CA buffer solutions (pH = 4.0–8.0, Table S1). As pH increased from 4.0 to 8.0, the fluorescence of CDs enhanced gradually (Fig. 2a), indicating the pH sensitivity of CDs. The intensity at 482 nm vs pH (Fig. 2b) was plotted and the obtained nonlinear curve was fitted to be y = 1038.66–12,280.88*e(−x/1.42) (R2 = 0.99), demonstrating that CDs are up-and-coming candidates for detecting pH in the range of 4.0 to 8.0. The curve of pH response versus FL intensity has a jumping range from pH 4.0 to 6.0 (Fig. 2b), and the pKa of CDs was 5.4 ± 0.13. Meanwhile, the color of CDs solution changed with pH variation, which could be easily saw with the naked eyes (Fig. 2b, inset). The FL intensity of CDs did not change significantly even in 0.5 M of NaCl solution, verifying CDs possess high ionic strength stability (Fig. S1a). In addition, the FL intensity remained almost constant under 8 h of irradiation of UV light, indicating that CDs had high photostability (Fig. S1b).

The zeta potentials of CDs displayed gradual lower tendency with increasing pH (Fig. S2), proving the deprotonation of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups in higher pH. The fluorescence was quenched under acidic conditions and recovered under alkaline conditions. In order to apply CDs for in vivo pH detection, we selected the physiological pH to study the reversibility of CDs (Fig. 2c). The circulating pH is adjusted by repeatedly dropping a small amount of acid or base into the solution to be tested. The results prove that CDs are reversible between pH 4.0 and 7.4. In the existence of various ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Li2+, 0.1 M) and biologically active molecules (Glucose, GSH, Cys, Asp, Gln, 5 mM), the FL intensity of CDs did not change significantly (Fig. 2d), proving that CDs have high selectivity toward H+ without the interference from other ions and bioactive molecules. Besides, a good nonlinear relationship between the pH and fluorescence of CDs was also achieved by in vivo optical imaging system (PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina Series III). As shown in Fig. 2e and f, the signal intensity of CDs reinforced proportionally with the raise of pH (4.0–7.4) and the nonlinear fitting equation was y = 5.84–227.69*e(−x/0.99), R2 = 0.99.

Before CDs were used as a pH fluorescence sensor for bioimaging, the cytotoxicities of CDs against HeLa cells were assessed by using the standard methyl thiazoletetrazolium (MTT) cell viability assay. As depicted in Fig. S3a, no distinct cytotoxicity was caused by incubation with CDs for 24 h, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of CDs. When HeLa cells were treated with CDs (20 μg/mL) for different time points, CDs presented time-dependent endocytosis and the intense green fluorescence was found at 4 h (Fig. S3b), indicating that CDs are promising for in vitro bioimaging. Furthermore, the zebrafish embryos that would hatch from the egg membrane were selected as vertebrate models to appraise the potential of CDs for in vivo imaging. After soaking zebrafish embryos with CDs (1 mg/mL) for 2 h, CDs were introduced into the embryos. As shown in Fig. S3c, for embryos that had not completely hatched from the egg membrane, CDs were mainly distributed in the egg membrane, while for embryos that were completely hatched from the egg membrane, CDs were mostly existed in the yolk sac. The distribution of CDs during embryonic development indicated that CDs had different tissue affinities. The prominent optical features of CDs is conducive to observe their biodistribution in zebrafish embryos, which verifies the practicability of CDs as an bioimaging probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Vitro Fluorescence Imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To certificate the feasibility of CDs for pH quantification in live cells, HeLa cells were incubated with 10 μg/mL of CDs for 3 h, then treated with different pH of DMEM (pH = 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0) for 1 h. The cells were observed by CLSM [33]. At pH 4.0, the FL signal was very week. By sharp contrast, HeLa cells emitted strong green fluorescence at pH 8.0 (Fig. 3a). There was obvious enhancement in FL intensity when pH increased from 4.0 to 8.0 (Fig. 3b). The good linear relationship was deduced to be y = 1.07× + 0.18 (R2 = 0.99) (Fig. 3c). The pKa of CDs in cells was 5.4 ± 0.53. CDs exhibited eximious pH optical response in living cells, and could be used as a probe to visualize intracellular pH fluctuations.

Inspired by the excellent performance of CDs in cells, we studied whether they could monitor pH fluctuation in small animals. First, we studied the pH sensing of CDs in juvenile zebrafish. In Fig. 4a, when juveniles zebrafish were incubated with CDs (1 mg/mL) solution (pH = 4.0–8.0) for 2 h, the green signal in juveniles was very weak at pH = 4.0 and significantly enhanced at pH = 8.0. In the pH = 4.0–8.0, the fluorescence heightened gradually with the rise of pH (Fig. 4b). CDs could supply the pH-dependent linear signals over the pH range of 4.0–8.0, the linear equation was fitted to be y = 2.33×-8.53, R2 = 0.99 (Fig. 4c). Moreover, we also applied CDs to detect the pH fluctuation of living adult zebrafish, and proved the universality of CDs pH sensor in zebrafish by using in vivo optical imaging system. In Fig. 4d, adult zebrafish was treated with CDs (1 mg/mL) for 2 h, a gradual increase in FL signal was discovered in the intestine and digestive system of adult zebrafish, as pH increased from 4.0 to 8.0. A relationship between signal intensity and pH was fitted to be y = 0.15× + 0.04, R2 = 0.99 (Fig. 4e). The results demonstrated CDs were very convinent for non-invasive and online pH quantitative detection of zebrafish.

Encouraged by the successful imaging of zebrafish, we chose mouse as another animal model to further evaluate the universality of CDs in monitoring pH changes in vivo. Different pH of CDs solution (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) was injected into the left lower limb of mice, and the signals were detected by in vivo optical imaging system. The FL signals remarkably went up with the pH increases from 4.0 to 7.4 (Fig. 5a). With the aid of quantitative image analysis, the linear relationship between pH and signal intensity was plotted as y = 0.37×-1.18, R2 = 0.98 (Fig. 5b). The results mentioned above confirmed the unique performance of CDs in quantificationally detecting pH in complex biological systems.

We made further exploratory efforts to appraise the practicability of CDs to visualize pH changes in the microenvironment around the solid tumor. CDs solution (1 mg/mL, 100 μL) was injected subcutaneously into the left (normal tissue) and right (U14 tumor tissue) lower limbs of the same mouse, respectively. The FL signal in the normal tissue was more powerful than that in the tumor tissue (Fig. 6a). The quantified intensity (Fig. 6b) indicated that the fluorescence of tumor tissue was lower than that of normal tissue. According to the standard curve of Fig. 5b, pH in the normal and tumor tissue was calculated to be 7.2 and 6.6, respectively, demonstrating the weak acidic microenvironment in the tumor tissue. Therefore, CDs are a powerful pH-activatable sensor for real-time tracking subtle pH changes in living cells, zebrafish and mice.

Highly efficient green fluorescent CDs were fabricated, which were pH-sensitive in a wide range (pH = 4.0–8.0). The strong points of high photostability, nice selectivity, and favorable biosafety impart CDs outstanding play in imaging and quantitatively sensing pH-fluctuations in living cells, zebrafish and mice. More importantly, CDs could quantitatively measuring pH variation in the tumor microenvironment. As far as we can tell, this is the first report on CDs-based nanoprobes for detecting pH alterations in tumors up to now. The present work provides a feasible strategy for on-line inspecting pH of complicated biological systems.
All animal experiments comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and the British Animal (Scientific Procedures) Act 1986 and related guidelines.

Libo Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Zhigang Xie: Resources, Supervision. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2022.112620.
